**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**WRITTEN EVIDENCE SUBMITTED BY THE CHRISTIAN INSTITUTE (OSB30)**

**ONLINE SAFETY BILL COMMITTEE**

**Introduction**

1.The Christian Institute exists for "the furtherance and promotion of the Christian religion in the United Kingdom". We are a non-denominational Christian charity operating throughout the UK. We are supported by approximately 60,000 individuals and churches. We have decades of experience defending free speech and religious liberty.

2.We welcome some of the provisions of this Bill, particularly the long overdue steps to prevent children accessing online pornography (although there are concerns about how well it will work as drafted – see Appendix 1) and the new cyberflashing offence. However, this submission focuses on four aspects of the Bill we do not support:

· The concept of ‘legal but harmful’, as applied to adults, is dangerously vague and will inevitably lead to free speech being restricted online;

· An unacceptable level of power is delegated to the Secretary of State that would allow the whole regulatory framework to be rewritten by statutory instrument;

· The Bill’s free speech protections are wholly inadequate;

· The ‘harmful communications’ offence is too broad and subjective.

**Concept of ‘legal but harmful’**

3.This concept, particularly with regard to adults, is at the heart of our concerns. It creates the potential for free speech to be far more restricted online than offline.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**WRITTEN EVIDENCE SUBMITTED BY THE CHRISTIAN INSTITUTE (OSB30)**

**ONLINE SAFETY BILL COMMITTEE**

**Introduction**

1.The Christian Institute exists for "the furtherance and promotion of the Christian religion in the United Kingdom". We are a non-denominational Christian charity operating throughout the UK. We are supported by approximately 60,000 individuals and churches. We have decades of experience defending free speech and religious liberty.

2.We welcome some of the provisions of this Bill, particularly the long overdue steps to prevent children accessing online pornography (although there are concerns about how well it will work as drafted – see Appendix 1) and the new cyberflashing offence. However, this submission focuses on four aspects of the Bill we do not support:

· The concept of ‘legal but harmful’, as applied to adults, is dangerously vague and will inevitably lead to free speech being restricted online;

· An unacceptable level of power is delegated to the Secretary of State that would allow the whole regulatory framework to be rewritten by statutory instrument;

· The Bill’s free speech protections are wholly inadequate;

· The ‘harmful communications’ offence is too broad and subjective.

**Concept of ‘legal but harmful’**

3.This concept, particularly with regard to adults, is at the heart of our concerns. It creates the potential for free speech to be far more restricted online than offline.

chunks.text: free speech protections are wholly inadequate; · The ‘harmful communications’ offence is too broad and subjective. **Concept of ‘legal but harmful’** 3.This concept, particularly with regard to adults, is at the heart of our concerns. It creates the potential for free speech to be far more restricted online than offline. 4.Offline, we have excellent free speech laws. What restrictions we have on public speech are usually necessary to protect the rights and freedoms of others. The criminal law is generally the arbiter of harm offline. If the Bill concentrated on illegal content then it would be far less problematic. Subject to the necessary adjustments to apply it to the online space, the criminal law must apply on the internet in the same way as offline. The Bill must require internet companies to prevent illegal content appearing on their platforms (although expecting these companies to reliably identify such content is a big ask – see Appendix 2). If there are loopholes, they need to be plugged. But the category of legal but harmful goes far beyond the criminal law. It imposes restrictions online that do not exist offline. 5.Everyone accepts that, as well as being protected from illegal content, children need additional protection from legal content that is clearly harmful to them because of their particular vulnerabilities. It is when the category of legal but harmful is extended to adults that it becomes dangerous.
chunks.display_text: 4.Offline, we have excellent free speech laws. What restrictions we have on public speech are usually necessary to protect the rights and freedoms of others. The criminal law is generally the arbiter of harm offline. If the Bill concentrated on illegal content then it would be far less problematic. Subject to the necessary adjustments to apply it to the online space, the criminal law must apply on the internet in the same way as offline. The Bill must require internet companies to prevent illegal content appearing on their platforms (although expecting these companies to reliably identify such content is a big ask – see Appendix 2). If there are loopholes, they need to be plugged. But the category of legal but harmful goes far beyond the criminal law. It imposes restrictions online that do not exist offline. 5.Everyone accepts that, as well as being protected from illegal content, children need additional protection from legal content that is clearly harmful to them because of their particular vulnerabilities. It is when the category of legal but harmful is extended to adults that it becomes dangerous.

chunks.text: do not exist offline. 5.Everyone accepts that, as well as being protected from illegal content, children need additional protection from legal content that is clearly harmful to them because of their particular vulnerabilities. It is when the category of legal but harmful is extended to adults that it becomes dangerous. 6.Free speech in the market square means that a person is free to say what they want within the law and others are equally free to put headphones on and ignore them. Free speech does not mean forcing others to listen. In many ways, social media platforms are the internet equivalent of the market square, and so free speech there must be equally protected. At the same time, more tools can be given to users so that they can choose to do the equivalent of putting on headphones.
chunks.display_text: 6.Free speech in the market square means that a person is free to say what they want within the law and others are equally free to put headphones on and ignore them. Free speech does not mean forcing others to listen. In many ways, social media platforms are the internet equivalent of the market square, and so free speech there must be equally protected. At the same time, more tools can be given to users so that they can choose to do the equivalent of putting on headphones.

chunks.text: others to listen. In many ways, social media platforms are the internet equivalent of the market square, and so free speech there must be equally protected. At the same time, more tools can be given to users so that they can choose to do the equivalent of putting on headphones. 7.Adults need to be equipped and enabled to make informed decisions about the content they are exposed to online. That requires transparency and the right tools. In places, the Bill recognises this. The emphasis on terms of service being clear and publicly available is designed to enhance transparency. Clause 57 is about making sure that all users, not just those in the public eye, can verify their accounts. Clause 14(6) would then mean anyone can decide whether to allow unverified users to interact with them. This will be a huge step in tackling the scourge of anonymous abuse online. The option to not see content from unverified users will provide the tools users need to avoid anonymous abuse. The same is true of Clause 14(2) about reducing exposure to content deemed harmful. These provisions increase the extent to which a user can tailor their online experience. This kind of personal choice is the right way to deal with adult content that is not illegal. Users can choose not to see certain content on the platform. But that does not mean the content cannot be on the platform at all. And those who want to be able to see everything other people post will also have that choice.
chunks.display_text: 7.Adults need to be equipped and enabled to make informed decisions about the content they are exposed to online. That requires transparency and the right tools. In places, the Bill recognises this. The emphasis on terms of service being clear and publicly available is designed to enhance transparency. Clause 57 is about making sure that all users, not just those in the public eye, can verify their accounts. Clause 14(6) would then mean anyone can decide whether to allow unverified users to interact with them. This will be a huge step in tackling the scourge of anonymous abuse online. The option to not see content from unverified users will provide the tools users need to avoid anonymous abuse. The same is true of Clause 14(2) about reducing exposure to content deemed harmful. These provisions increase the extent to which a user can tailor their online experience. This kind of personal choice is the right way to deal with adult content that is not illegal. Users can choose not to see certain content on the platform. But that does not mean the content cannot be on the platform at all. And those who want to be able to see everything other people post will also have that choice.

chunks.text: with adult content that is not illegal. Users can choose not to see certain content on the platform. But that does not mean the content cannot be on the platform at all. And those who want to be able to see everything other people post will also have that choice. 8.If something is so harmful that adults should not be exposed to it, this should be a matter for the criminal law. Short of that, adults should be able to make their own decisions. The Government should not be creating a nebulous ‘legal but harmful’ standard and then outsourcing the policing of that standard to Ofcom and tech firms. 9.The danger of the vague legal but harmful threshold is compounded by the fact that it will be enforced by algorithms. Big Tech firms will not take on the staff to enable them to comply with all the additional duties via human decision making. It would not be practical or effective because the task is so huge. Instead, they will have to use algorithms, which are blunt instruments and will be cautiously designed to over-filter. It is inevitable that this will result in false positives and unjustified restrictions on content. Ø **The concept of legal but harmful to adults should be removed from the Bill, putting the focus instead on giving adults the tools to make their own decisions about what they see.** **Extent of the delegated powers**
chunks.display_text: 8.If something is so harmful that adults should not be exposed to it, this should be a matter for the criminal law. Short of that, adults should be able to make their own decisions. The Government should not be creating a nebulous ‘legal but harmful’ standard and then outsourcing the policing of that standard to Ofcom and tech firms. 9.The danger of the vague legal but harmful threshold is compounded by the fact that it will be enforced by algorithms. Big Tech firms will not take on the staff to enable them to comply with all the additional duties via human decision making. It would not be practical or effective because the task is so huge. Instead, they will have to use algorithms, which are blunt instruments and will be cautiously designed to over-filter. It is inevitable that this will result in false positives and unjustified restrictions on content. Ø **The concept of legal but harmful to adults should be removed from the Bill, putting the focus instead on giving adults the tools to make their own decisions about what they see.** **Extent of the delegated powers**

chunks.text: that this will result in false positives and unjustified restrictions on content. Ø **The concept of legal but harmful to adults should be removed from the Bill, putting the focus instead on giving adults the tools to make their own decisions about what they see.** **Extent of the delegated powers** 10.As well as our concerns about the principle of the legal but harmful standard, the power of the Secretary of State over the scope of this category is a serious problem. The detail of what is legal but harmful will be left to secondary legislation. In the absence of this detail, Parliament is being asked to vote through legislation without knowing what it will and will not censor. There seem to be no clear boundaries on what could be banned in future. 11.To make scrutiny more meaningful, the Secretary of State should publish drafts of the proposed secondary legislation now. But there is little to limit the scope of future secondary legislation other than the views of the Secretary of State of the day. 12.Clause 55 says that content can be listed as a priority legal harm if the Secretary of State considers it to present "a material risk of significant harm to an appreciable number of adults". · ‘Risk of significant harm’ is a threshold that has operated in child protection for decades. It reflects the particular vulnerability of children. It is inappropriate to apply it to adults. The Director of Defend Digital Me, Jen Persson, has raised the concern that by considering all adults to be at risk of harm "the bill infantilises us all". \[1\]
chunks.display_text: 10.As well as our concerns about the principle of the legal but harmful standard, the power of the Secretary of State over the scope of this category is a serious problem. The detail of what is legal but harmful will be left to secondary legislation. In the absence of this detail, Parliament is being asked to vote through legislation without knowing what it will and will not censor. There seem to be no clear boundaries on what could be banned in future. 11.To make scrutiny more meaningful, the Secretary of State should publish drafts of the proposed secondary legislation now. But there is little to limit the scope of future secondary legislation other than the views of the Secretary of State of the day. 12.Clause 55 says that content can be listed as a priority legal harm if the Secretary of State considers it to present "a material risk of significant harm to an appreciable number of adults". · ‘Risk of significant harm’ is a threshold that has operated in child protection for decades. It reflects the particular vulnerability of children. It is inappropriate to apply it to adults. The Director of Defend Digital Me, Jen Persson, has raised the concern that by considering all adults to be at risk of harm "the bill infantilises us all". \[1\]

chunks.text: operated in child protection for decades. It reflects the particular vulnerability of children. It is inappropriate to apply it to adults. The Director of Defend Digital Me, Jen Persson, has raised the concern that by considering all adults to be at risk of harm "the bill infantilises us all". \[1\] · There is obvious ambiguity in "an appreciable number". Comically, the Explanatory Notes to the Bill define it as between very few and not very large: "content need not affect very large numbers of people to be considered harmful, but kinds of content which affect only one person or very few people are not in scope". · The Bill has no requirement that the adults at risk are, for example, reasonable or of ordinary sensibilities. This runs the risk of content being specified as priority legal harm on the basis of an abnormally sensitive group of people. **The draft Bill used the standard of "an adult of ordinary sensibilities", an addition that would improve the current Bill. Even better phrasing would be "a *reasonable* adult of ordinary sensibilities".** 13.So far, the suggestion has been that the priority legal harms will include matters like self-harm, harassment, eating disorders and racist hate. \[2\] We note that harassment and racial hatred are already criminal offences. It is not clear why promoting self-harm should not also be made a criminal offence, online and offline. Whereas, Clause 13(4) bizarrely envisages that all of these things are content that a social media company could choose to recommend or promote.
chunks.display_text: · There is obvious ambiguity in "an appreciable number". Comically, the Explanatory Notes to the Bill define it as between very few and not very large: "content need not affect very large numbers of people to be considered harmful, but kinds of content which affect only one person or very few people are not in scope". · The Bill has no requirement that the adults at risk are, for example, reasonable or of ordinary sensibilities. This runs the risk of content being specified as priority legal harm on the basis of an abnormally sensitive group of people. **The draft Bill used the standard of "an adult of ordinary sensibilities", an addition that would improve the current Bill. Even better phrasing would be "a *reasonable* adult of ordinary sensibilities".** 13.So far, the suggestion has been that the priority legal harms will include matters like self-harm, harassment, eating disorders and racist hate. \[2\] We note that harassment and racial hatred are already criminal offences. It is not clear why promoting self-harm should not also be made a criminal offence, online and offline. Whereas, Clause 13(4) bizarrely envisages that all of these things are content that a social media company could choose to recommend or promote.

chunks.text: that harassment and racial hatred are already criminal offences. It is not clear why promoting self-harm should not also be made a criminal offence, online and offline. Whereas, Clause 13(4) bizarrely envisages that all of these things are content that a social media company could choose to recommend or promote. 14.Whatever the initial scope of priority legal harms, any Secretary of State would be able to amend it subsequently by regulations. The affirmative procedure would be used, usually meaning in the Commons a 90-minute debate in a delegated legislation committee with any vote on a take-it-or-leave-it basis with no scope for amendments. Considering the potential of such regulations to restrict free speech, this would be completely inadequate scrutiny. The boundaries of online debate must not be determined by government decree. Previous laws with implications for free speech have rightly been in primary legislation, sometimes only after years of scrutiny. For example, the religious hatred offence took five years across three different Bills before Parliament was willing to pass it. \[3\] Instead of years, it will be minutes of scrutiny under the powers created by this Bill. 15.At the very least, the super-affirmative procedure should be used, to give opportunity for feedback and amendment to a draft before the measure is formally laid. It would be far better to require the Secretary of State to consult the public on the draft. 16.Defining legal but harmful is just one of the regulation-making powers in the Bill that hand unacceptable control to the Secretary of State. The delegated powers memorandum admits that there are 19 delegated powers to amend primary legislation (so-called "Henry VIII powers"). \[4\] 17.The Secretary of State’s powers include: · changing the overarching online safety objectives set out in the legislation (Schedule 4);

The affirmative procedure would be used, usually meaning in the Commons a 90-minute debate in a delegated legislation committee with any vote on a take-it-or-leave-it basis with no scope for amendments. Considering the potential of such regulations to restrict free speech, this would be completely inadequate scrutiny. The boundaries of online debate must not be determined by government decree. Previous laws with implications for free speech have rightly been in primary legislation, sometimes only after years of scrutiny. For example, the religious hatred offence took five years across three different Bills before Parliament was willing to pass it. \[3\] Instead of years, it will be minutes of scrutiny under the powers created by this Bill. 15.At the very least, the super-affirmative procedure should be used, to give opportunity for feedback and amendment to a draft before the measure is formally laid. It would be far better to require the Secretary of State to consult the public on the draft. 16.Defining legal but harmful is just one of the regulation-making powers in the Bill that hand unacceptable control to the Secretary of State. The delegated powers memorandum admits that there are 19 delegated powers to amend primary legislation (so-called "Henry VIII powers"). \[4\] 17.The Secretary of State’s powers include: · changing the overarching online safety objectives set out in the legislation (Schedule 4);

chunks.text: Bill that hand unacceptable control to the Secretary of State. The delegated powers memorandum admits that there are 19 delegated powers to amend primary legislation (so-called "Henry VIII powers"). \[4\] 17.The Secretary of State’s powers include: · changing the overarching online safety objectives set out in the legislation (Schedule 4); · specifying priority content to be targeted – of which ‘legal but harmful’ is just one part (Clauses 53-55 and 176); · giving directions and guidance to Ofcom (Clauses 40 and 147); · altering content and services that are exempt from regulation (Clause 174); and · deciding who can complain to Ofcom (Clause 140). 18.Virtually all the substantive parts of the regime could be changed by regulations. Even the emphasis and strategic objectives of the law could be amended. This is extraordinary. Parliament should not pass legislation which can be wholly revised by statutory instrument. Ø **If the legal but harmful standard is to be retained, the detail of what it covers should be on the face of the Bill, not left to secondary legislation.** Ø **There should be a drastic reduction in the powers given to the Secretary of State.** **Inadequate protection for free speech** 19.The Government has realised that the power wielded by social media decision makers is a serious problem.\[5\] But the Bill does not include the robust free speech protections required for it to be the solution to this problem.
chunks.display_text: · specifying priority content to be targeted – of which ‘legal but harmful’ is just one part (Clauses 53-55 and 176); · giving directions and guidance to Ofcom (Clauses 40 and 147); · altering content and services that are exempt from regulation (Clause 174); and · deciding who can complain to Ofcom (Clause 140). 18.Virtually all the substantive parts of the regime could be changed by regulations. Even the emphasis and strategic objectives of the law could be amended. This is extraordinary. Parliament should not pass legislation which can be wholly revised by statutory instrument. Ø **If the legal but harmful standard is to be retained, the detail of what it covers should be on the face of the Bill, not left to secondary legislation.** Ø **There should be a drastic reduction in the powers given to the Secretary of State.** **Inadequate protection for free speech** 19.The Government has realised that the power wielded by social media decision makers is a serious problem.\[5\] But the Bill does not include the robust free speech protections required for it to be the solution to this problem.

chunks.text: powers given to the Secretary of State.** **Inadequate protection for free speech** 19.The Government has realised that the power wielded by social media decision makers is a serious problem.\[5\] But the Bill does not include the robust free speech protections required for it to be the solution to this problem. 20.We were promised "considerably stronger protections for free speech" but the Bill does not deliver. Internet companies will be under "a duty to have regard to the importance of" protecting free speech. Such a ‘have regard’ duty has no weight behind it. It is perfectly possible to take have regard to something in theory and then ignore it in practice. It is certainly not strong enough to counterbalance the way other substantial duties restrict free speech. 21.There should be a strong statutory presumption in favour of freedom of expression. Whatever you can say on the street you should be able to say online. Companies that remove or restrict content should have to justify overturning the presumption in that case. Ofcom should also be put under a proactive duty to take all reasonable steps to uphold the principle that what is legal to say offline is also allowed online. 22.Free speech at universities is an interesting comparison. Universities are legally required to take reasonable steps "to ensure freedom of speech within the law is secured". \[6\] It is extraordinary that at the same time as free speech at universities is being bolstered, free speech online is being undermined. The universities free speech provision would be a good starting point for an online free speech duty applying to all services.
chunks.display_text: 20.We were promised "considerably stronger protections for free speech" but the Bill does not deliver. Internet companies will be under "a duty to have regard to the importance of" protecting free speech. Such a ‘have regard’ duty has no weight behind it. It is perfectly possible to take have regard to something in theory and then ignore it in practice. It is certainly not strong enough to counterbalance the way other substantial duties restrict free speech. 21.There should be a strong statutory presumption in favour of freedom of expression. Whatever you can say on the street you should be able to say online. Companies that remove or restrict content should have to justify overturning the presumption in that case. Ofcom should also be put under a proactive duty to take all reasonable steps to uphold the principle that what is legal to say offline is also allowed online. 22.Free speech at universities is an interesting comparison. Universities are legally required to take reasonable steps "to ensure freedom of speech within the law is secured". \[6\] It is extraordinary that at the same time as free speech at universities is being bolstered, free speech online is being undermined. The universities free speech provision would be a good starting point for an online free speech duty applying to all services.

chunks.text: speech within the law is secured". \[6\] It is extraordinary that at the same time as free speech at universities is being bolstered, free speech online is being undermined. The universities free speech provision would be a good starting point for an online free speech duty applying to all services. 23.The inadequacy of the general free speech provision in Clause 19 is evident from the fact that the Government felt it necessary to give additional protection to journalists (Clause 16) and for content of democratic importance (Clause 15). The existence of these additional clauses is effectively an admission that the rest of the Bill really does threaten free speech and that Clause 19 is not up to the job. In any event Clause 15 is too narrowly drawn. In focusing its definition of "content of democratic importance" on contributions to "democratic political debate" it risks excluding whole swathes of debate which many would consider just as important. And why should journalists have a right to say things that the rest of us can’t? 24.The Government claims a stronger free speech duty is not possible because of the common law free speech rights of companies. They say companies "are free to decide what content should and should not be on their website within the bounds of the law". \[7\] Yet the whole Bill is about setting the bounds of the law and putting additional duties on internet companies regarding content. 25.The largest internet companies are competitor-swallowing cartels that have enormous influence and this is why the Bill has been brought forward. The Government has concluded that they cannot be trusted with users’ safety. Neither should they be trusted with free speech.
chunks.display_text: 23.The inadequacy of the general free speech provision in Clause 19 is evident from the fact that the Government felt it necessary to give additional protection to journalists (Clause 16) and for content of democratic importance (Clause 15). The existence of these additional clauses is effectively an admission that the rest of the Bill really does threaten free speech and that Clause 19 is not up to the job. In any event Clause 15 is too narrowly drawn. In focusing its definition of "content of democratic importance" on contributions to "democratic political debate" it risks excluding whole swathes of debate which many would consider just as important. And why should journalists have a right to say things that the rest of us can’t? 24.The Government claims a stronger free speech duty is not possible because of the common law free speech rights of companies. They say companies "are free to decide what content should and should not be on their website within the bounds of the law". \[7\] Yet the whole Bill is about setting the bounds of the law and putting additional duties on internet companies regarding content. 25.The largest internet companies are competitor-swallowing cartels that have enormous influence and this is why the Bill has been brought forward. The Government has concluded that they cannot be trusted with users’ safety. Neither should they be trusted with free speech.

chunks.text: and putting additional duties on internet companies regarding content. 25.The largest internet companies are competitor-swallowing cartels that have enormous influence and this is why the Bill has been brought forward. The Government has concluded that they cannot be trusted with users’ safety. Neither should they be trusted with free speech. 26.We would all recognise the remarkable danger our culture would be in if 80% of printing presses were owned by one firm. Yet this is the level of dominance some companies are reaching. The content they carry – and do not carry – can profoundly affect the terms of popular debate. 27.Given this near-monopoly power, such companies must be under corresponding responsibilities as to how that power is used. It is part of their social obligations. Requiring social media companies to ensure free speech is secured within the law is perfectly reasonable, and consistent with being the forums for sharing ideas and information they purport to be. 28.The Government has trumpeted the Clause 19(4) duty on service providers to make clear to users that they can sue for breach of contract if content is taken down or restricted contrary to the terms of service. Yet there are at least two fundamental problems with this. Firstly, the idea that the average user has the financial capacity to go up against internet giants in court is laughable. And, secondly, such legal action would require the terms of service to have been breached. This will encourage companies to give themselves *carte blanche* in their terms of service to prevent such legal claims being brought. Ø **The Bill must include far stronger free speech protections, whether a presumption in favour of free speech or a duty like the one for universities "to ensure freedom of speech within the law is secured".** **Harmful communications offence**
chunks.display_text: 26.We would all recognise the remarkable danger our culture would be in if 80% of printing presses were owned by one firm. Yet this is the level of dominance some companies are reaching. The content they carry – and do not carry – can profoundly affect the terms of popular debate. 27.Given this near-monopoly power, such companies must be under corresponding responsibilities as to how that power is used. It is part of their social obligations. Requiring social media companies to ensure free speech is secured within the law is perfectly reasonable, and consistent with being the forums for sharing ideas and information they purport to be. 28.The Government has trumpeted the Clause 19(4) duty on service providers to make clear to users that they can sue for breach of contract if content is taken down or restricted contrary to the terms of service. Yet there are at least two fundamental problems with this. Firstly, the idea that the average user has the financial capacity to go up against internet giants in court is laughable. And, secondly, such legal action would require the terms of service to have been breached. This will encourage companies to give themselves *carte blanche* in their terms of service to prevent such legal claims being brought. Ø **The Bill must include far stronger free speech protections, whether a presumption in favour of free speech or a duty like the one for universities "to ensure freedom of speech within the law is secured".** **Harmful communications offence**

chunks.text: their terms of service to prevent such legal claims being brought. Ø **The Bill must include far stronger free speech protections, whether a presumption in favour of free speech or a duty like the one for universities "to ensure freedom of speech within the law is secured".** **Harmful communications offence** 29.The offence in Clause 150 is remarkably broad and subjective. It catches sending a message with "a real and substantial risk that it would cause harm to a likely audience", with the intention of causing such harm and without reasonable excuse. It carries a potential two-year prison term. 30."Likely audience" includes those whom it is reasonably foreseeable would encounter the message through sharing or forwarding. For the purposes of this offence, "harm" is defined as psychological harm, amounting to at least serious distress. 31.Distress is a subjective term, and even "serious distress" could be a dangerously low threshold. No distress need actually be caused. The real audience, as opposed to the theoretical ‘likely audience’, may be completely unaffected by the content. 32.We see many examples today of people taking offence easily and claiming to be harmed by opinions they disagree with in order to shut them down. For example, students at Exeter University sought to have the pro-life society banned because of its "harmful point of view". \[8\] J K Rowling’s defence of the reality and importance of biological sex has been labelled "harmful". \[9\] 33.An offence targeting psychological harm risks putting a weapon in the hands of those who claim to be harmed by another’s speech. They will be able to contact the police and allege they are being harmed, because ‘harm’ is effectively in the eye of the beholder.
chunks.display_text: 29.The offence in Clause 150 is remarkably broad and subjective. It catches sending a message with "a real and substantial risk that it would cause harm to a likely audience", with the intention of causing such harm and without reasonable excuse. It carries a potential two-year prison term. 30."Likely audience" includes those whom it is reasonably foreseeable would encounter the message through sharing or forwarding. For the purposes of this offence, "harm" is defined as psychological harm, amounting to at least serious distress. 31.Distress is a subjective term, and even "serious distress" could be a dangerously low threshold. No distress need actually be caused. The real audience, as opposed to the theoretical ‘likely audience’, may be completely unaffected by the content. 32.We see many examples today of people taking offence easily and claiming to be harmed by opinions they disagree with in order to shut them down. For example, students at Exeter University sought to have the pro-life society banned because of its "harmful point of view". \[8\] J K Rowling’s defence of the reality and importance of biological sex has been labelled "harmful". \[9\] 33.An offence targeting psychological harm risks putting a weapon in the hands of those who claim to be harmed by another’s speech. They will be able to contact the police and allege they are being harmed, because ‘harm’ is effectively in the eye of the beholder.

chunks.text: been labelled "harmful". \[9\] 33.An offence targeting psychological harm risks putting a weapon in the hands of those who claim to be harmed by another’s speech. They will be able to contact the police and allege they are being harmed, because ‘harm’ is effectively in the eye of the beholder. 34.The danger of relying on the perception of the victim has been exposed by the growth of non-crime hate incidents, and the Government is taking steps to crack down on this waste of police resources. Yet creating a new offence based on subjective standards of harm will only encourage more complaints about trivial online speech. Dragging the police into Twitter spats about contentious issues is a waste of valuable resources. As the new Chief Inspector of Constabulary has recently pointed out: "Policing is busy enough dealing with the serious offences that are going on, busy enough trying to keep people safe." \[10\] Ø **Clause 150 should be dropped, unless it can be substantially redrafted in such a way as to be narrower and less subjective (which seems unlikely using the concept of ‘harm’). ** **APPENDIX 1 – Age assurance for online pornography** While in principle we welcome the age verification provisions for pornography included in the Bill, we share the reservations highlighted by CARE, namely: 1.There are **significant inconsistencies** between the requirements on platforms that contain user-to-user generated pornographic content (Part 3) and dedicated pornographic websites (Part 5). For example, while Part 3 requires social media companies to remove illegal pornography, Part 5 will simply require dedicated websites to restrict access to it. **There needs to be consistent treatment for all online platforms containing pornography and all content regulated offline should also be regulated online**.
chunks.display_text: 34.The danger of relying on the perception of the victim has been exposed by the growth of non-crime hate incidents, and the Government is taking steps to crack down on this waste of police resources. Yet creating a new offence based on subjective standards of harm will only encourage more complaints about trivial online speech. Dragging the police into Twitter spats about contentious issues is a waste of valuable resources. As the new Chief Inspector of Constabulary has recently pointed out: "Policing is busy enough dealing with the serious offences that are going on, busy enough trying to keep people safe." \[10\] Ø **Clause 150 should be dropped, unless it can be substantially redrafted in such a way as to be narrower and less subjective (which seems unlikely using the concept of ‘harm’). ** **APPENDIX 1 – Age assurance for online pornography** While in principle we welcome the age verification provisions for pornography included in the Bill, we share the reservations highlighted by CARE, namely: 1.There are **significant inconsistencies** between the requirements on platforms that contain user-to-user generated pornographic content (Part 3) and dedicated pornographic websites (Part 5). For example, while Part 3 requires social media companies to remove illegal pornography, Part 5 will simply require dedicated websites to restrict access to it. **There needs to be consistent treatment for all online platforms containing pornography and all content regulated offline should also be regulated online**.

chunks.text: websites (Part 5). For example, while Part 3 requires social media companies to remove illegal pornography, Part 5 will simply require dedicated websites to restrict access to it. **There needs to be consistent treatment for all online platforms containing pornography and all content regulated offline should also be regulated online**. 2.There is no clarity about what will be required to prevent children accessing pornographic content through **age verification.** Parts 3 and 5 of the Bill contradict each other over the requirements for Parliamentary scrutiny on the codes of practice. 3.There are key questions left unanswered about the **effectiveness of the enforcement regime** proposed in Part 7, including how the Government could collect fines from organisations outside the UK. 4.**Commencement arrangements** for Parts 3 and 5 could delay the protection of children yet further. Commencement dates should be stated on the face of the Bill. **APPENDIX 2 – Practical difficulties with illegal content duties** Meetings with DCMS officials about how social media companies could meet the illegal content duties have given rise to concern that the Bill’s expectations are unrealistic. Clause 9 of the Bill, particularly subsection (5), makes it clear that a core concept is content that "the provider reasonably considers is illegal content or a particular kind of illegal content". It is such content that the provider will be expected to restrict. DCMS officials have suggested this will include social media companies reaching a view on the *mens rea* of users posting content. For some offences and content, this exercise might be straightforward for providers. But for other offences, particular those touching on speech, it will be more difficult for providers to make judgements on whether particular content can reasonably be considered illegal.

Commencement dates should be stated on the face of the Bill. **APPENDIX 2 – Practical difficulties with illegal content duties** Meetings with DCMS officials about how social media companies could meet the illegal content duties have given rise to concern that the Bill’s expectations are unrealistic. Clause 9 of the Bill, particularly subsection (5), makes it clear that a core concept is content that "the provider reasonably considers is illegal content or a particular kind of illegal content". It is such content that the provider will be expected to restrict. DCMS officials have suggested this will include social media companies reaching a view on the *mens rea* of users posting content. For some offences and content, this exercise might be straightforward for providers. But for other offences, particular those touching on speech, it will be more difficult for providers to make judgements on whether particular content can reasonably be considered illegal.

chunks.text: a view on the *mens rea* of users posting content. For some offences and content, this exercise might be straightforward for providers. But for other offences, particular those touching on speech, it will be more difficult for providers to make judgements on whether particular content can reasonably be considered illegal. For example, the priority illegal content set out in Schedule 7 includes public order offences like sections 4A and 5 of the Public Order Act 1986. Although the police mostly get it right, they sometimes – despite their training and expertise – overstep the mark in enforcing these laws. This leads to unjustifiable arrests of street preachers, protestors and others. Asking tech companies to take parallel decisions, multiplied many times by the sheer number of social media posts compared to offline exchanges, is deeply problematic. Social media companies adjudicating on what constitutes a breach of public order will result in an exponentially greater number of cases of free speech being restricted. Schedule 7 also mentions sections 29B, 29C and 29E of the Public Order Act, which deal with stirring up hatred on the grounds of religion and sexual orientation. These are criminal offences covering speech, with a seven-year maximum custodial penalty. They are offences so serious and potentially restrictive of free speech that only the Attorney General can authorise a prosecution. There are weighty matters of law that need to be considered. The offences also contain several other free speech safeguards. Does the Bill expect social media company moderators to be fully versed in the nuances of such provisions?

The offences also contain several other free speech safeguards. Does the Bill expect social media company moderators to be fully versed in the nuances of such provisions? Moreover, as stated at paragraph 9, this exercise will not be carried out by staff but through automated processes like algorithms. These cannot be expected to apply the law effectively. They do not recognise sarcasm, irony or a host of other factors that are crucial to the context and therefore how content should be understood. So, just as with the ‘legal but harmful’ duty, there is a serious risk that the illegal content duties will lead to over-filtering, i.e. filtering out content that is actually perfectly legal, to the detriment of freedom of expression. *May 2022* --- \[1\] ‘The online safety bill will do little to protect children’, Jen Persson, *Schools Week,* 28 March 2022, see https://schoolsweek.co.uk/the-online-safety-bill-will-do-little-to-protect-children/ as at 12 April 2022 \[2\] ‘World-first online safety laws introduced in Parliament’, *UK Government*, 17 March 2022, see https://www.gov.uk/government/news/world-first-online-safety-laws-introduced-in-parliament as at 12 April 2022; ‘New plans to protect people from anonymous trolls online’, *UK Government,* 25 February 2022, see https://www.gov.uk/government/news/new-plans-to-protect-people-from-anonymous-trolls-online as at 12 April 2022 \[3\] 2001-2006: Anti - terrorism, Crime and Security Bill 2001; Serious Organised Crime and Police Bill 2004-5; Racial and Religious Hatred Act 2006 \[4\] Online Safety Bill: Memorandum from the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport and the Home Office to the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee, 17 March 2022, page 3
